data.title: IYRI Vs. VNQ: Active Strategy Outperforms Passive Indexing
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Funds with the largest AUM are not necessarily the clear leaders in their segment. Today, there are many new funds from experienced management teams that offer more attractive investment options in income-generating assets in terms of total return. This is confirmed by the case of the NEOS Real Estate High Income ETF (IYRI), since the yield of this ETF over the past year has outperformed the results of one of the REIT benchmarks, Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ). Admittedly, while the Vanguard fund has been in existence for nearly 22 years and weathered the 2008 crisis, NEOS has only been operating for just over a year, yet the latter team’s professionalism is already well-known to many investors based on their track record with other funds.

When comparing IYRI and VNQ, my intention is to determine which approach is more effective: actively managing a portfolio using options or simply tracking the index. For investors whose goal is to generate high monthly returns, IYRI is clearly the better choice. But even for active young investors like me, a fund from NEOS looks more appealing, since those monthly payments deposited into a brokerage account can be used for rapid reinvestment to increase current positions or purchase new assets.

In determining the difference between IYRI and VNQ, the outlook for each fund must be assessed in light of the current environment for the REIT sector. Because VNQ's portfolio reflects the broader market, this fund's performance depends on the yield curve and macroeconomic conditions. As long as the conflict in the Middle East does not drag on and energy prices return to lower levels, Fed rates will remain stable. In the alternative, a potential rate hike could occur, reducing the valuation of assets in the VNQ portfolio. As such, performance of the Vanguard portfolio is heavily dependent on whether rate stability persists and a strong bullish trend resumes.

Meanwhile, IYRI's portfolio management approach allows for the selection of assets that are better suited to generating additional returns from current market volatility. Covered call strategies improve the risk-return profile by providing portfolio protection during periods of stagnation or range-bound trading. The premiums received from regularly selling options generate additional returns for investors that significantly exceed VNQ’s payouts. The choice between these funds, thus, is dependent on the expected scenario for the REIT market. Given that the period of high volatility persists, the use of the NEOS fund is more logical, offering monetization of uncertainty and delivering higher returns from payouts.

Forecast Thesis on REITs

The basis of my forecast thesis is that the total return profile of funds such as IYRI and VNQ will remain more stable than that of assets tied to the performance of the S&P 500 (SPY) and Nasdaq-100 (QQQ) stock indices. This is because there are no signs of a real de-escalation of the conflict in the Middle East, maintaining the previous risks of rising energy prices, higher inflation expectations, and rising Treasury yields. The forecast is also based on an analysis of the current performance of REIT sector funds against stock market benchmarks. Since the beginning of the year, SPY and QQQ have posted negative total returns of approximately -4%, but IYRI and VNQ have shown the opposite trend, growing by approximately 2%. This difference between REIT assets and stock market benchmarks ranges from 5.21% to 7.71%, indicating the advantage of REIT funds in conditions of heightened market volatility.

We should note that the fear index (VIX) is showing abnormally high levels, which confirms that investors’ appetite for risky assets is waning. Consequently, outflows of capital from the broader stock market and those sectors are driving the negative performance of key assets. On the contrary, REITs are demonstrating steady profitability, since they combine historically low beta valuations relative to S&P 500 volatility with the stabilization of the Fed’s interest rate. Once the tech sector market became overbought, an outflow of capital began toward more tangible assets trading at a discount to NAV of up to 17% of the median. It is also worth noting the shortage of new supply in key real estate niches, including data centers and warehouses, due to which landlords are achieving high occupancy rates. Since the Fed is in a phase of holding interest rates steady, the fixed payments from REITs are becoming more attractive against the backdrop of higher volatility in growth stocks.

Fundamental Similarities Between IYRI and VNQ

Regardless of which ETF is considered better between IYRI and VNQ, it is worth noting their common similarities first.

  • Firstly, both funds' underlying assets are U.S. REITs engaged in the business of managing income-producing real estate.
  • Secondly, these funds are designed to generate passive income for distribution to investors, given that real estate is considered one of the most dividend-friendly sectors of the stock market.
  • Finally, portfolios of both funds are sensitive to interest rates, US macroeconomic conditions, as well as the dynamics of demand for real estate rentals.

Fundamental Differences Between IYRI and VNQ

Further analysis of these ETFs reveals the criteria by which they should be distinguished. In analyzing IYRI, one should note that this fund employs an active management strategy, which focuses not only on holding REIT shares but also on selling call options on them. Applied by the NEOS team across all its funds, it allows for the generation of additional income from option premiums on top of regular dividends. As market volatility in the REIT sector increases, the returns from the option strategy rise. This makes IYRI ideally suited for market conditions characterized by a sideways range with heightened volatility. However, in a market phase of aggressive stock growth, a "growth ceiling" problem arises. This means IYRI could potentially underperform its benchmark during a strong bull market. In fact, IYRI's portfolio is more concentrated, since it's made up of 64 holdings (the top 10 assets account for 51.41%). As the fund uses an options strategy, the management fee is above the median at 0.68%.

VNQ has a passive management style, since its portfolio is formed by tracking the MSCI US Investable Market Real Estate 25/50 Index. Shares of REITs are purchased in proportion to the market capitalization of the holdings. Since it is an index fund, the REIT sector is broadly covered, with 146 holdings included in the portfolio. But the concentration of the top 10 holdings remains high at 54.08%, up 2.67% from IYRI. Because the fund has no additional strategies for generating returns beyond the base REIT dividends, its main objective also includes total capital growth. Passive management makes this fund very inexpensive, charged at 0.12%.

Analysing the difference in dividend policies, note that the funds' payouts vary significantly. Because IYRI aims to generate high returns, the dividend payout stands at 11.08%. In addition to payouts, VNQ also aims for overall capital growth, with the yield itself derived from REIT dividends. As a result, the yield is three times lower, at 3.63%. Also worth noting is that IYRI pays dividends monthly, whereas VNQ pays quarterly.

To compare the total return profiles of the funds, let's look at the period starting January 14, 2025, the date the NEOS fund was launched. While the IYRI and VNQ perform quite similarly, the former is still 1.27% more profitable. In analyzing the tax efficiency of the funds, we should note that IYRI falls under Section 1256, which means that 60% of its income is taxed as long-term capital gains and 40% as short-term. Moreover, Form 8937 for 2025 indicates that 22% of IYRI’s distributions were in the form return of capital, allowing for deferral of taxation on a portion of the dividends until the shares are sold. But according to VNQ’s Form 8937 for 2025, that fund also pays dividends, where the share of RoC is about the same. From $3.47 in dividends for all of 2025, around $0.85 was returned as RoC.

The table below provides a more detailed comparison of IYRI and VNQ.

Investment Thesis Risks

Assessing the risks of investing in REITs, note that this sector may face the following challenges in 2026:

  • The stagnation of prices in certain U.S. states is having a potentially negative impact on residential REITs, potentially reducing future dividend yields.
  • Although interest rates have stabilized, the Fed's moves to tighten monetary policy would put pressure on mortgage loans, increasing the cost of debt servicing for REITs. A key factor in determining the direction of interest rate changes remains inflation.
  • REITs have become highly heterogeneous, with logistics and data center properties accounting for an increasing share of the market alongside office and residential real estate. It increases the differentiation of fund portfolios, and also increases the risk that problems in any single sector could trigger a general market decline in REITs.

Analyzing the individual risks of ETFs, worth noting is that the main risk for VNQ stems from a lack of hedging mechanisms against a potential downturn in the sector. Another risk to consider is a slow outflow of capital if inflation exceeds the fund’s dividend yield. IYRI's main risk is a growth ceiling, since the sale of call options hedges the portfolio, setting a limit beyond which the fund cannot outperform the market. That said, since REITs are a less volatile sector, IYRI faces a lower risk relative to other NEOS funds.

Conclusion

In 2026, REIT funds are among the most reliable and promising assets for income-oriented investing, given that the broader stock market is experiencing a downturn driven by market volatility and escalating geopolitical and inflationary risks. Compared to the VNQ, IYRI has a higher total return profile, while the NEOS product specifically offers high monthly returns from distributions. Taking into account that I am interested in more actively managed funds capable of generating additional returns from option selling premiums, I would recommend "Buy" for IYRI and "Hold" for VNQ. I also believe that the REIT sector is unlikely to experience a phase of rapid growth, meaning that the covered call strategy can provide IYRI investors with additional monetization of volatility in a market resembling a broad sideways trend. Given that options are more expensive during periods of market volatility, IYRI's yield has upside potential, whilst VNQ's portfolio simply records paper losses on NAV.

data.title: VMBS: Hold Rating Reflects Adequate Carry With A Negative Convexity Tax
data.description:
The Vanguard Mortgage-Backed Securities ETF (VMBS) provides passive exposure to U.S. agency mortgage pass-through securities issued by GNMA, FNMA, and FHLMC via the Bloomberg U.S. MBS Float Adjusted Index. At a 0.03% expense ratio, it is the lowest-cost agency MBS fund on the market. The fund holds over 5,000 individual pool aggregates, trades approximately 1.9 million shares daily, and currently sits at roughly $14.9 billion in AUM after a notable institutional rebalancing event in early 2025.

This fund does a great job of providing passive access to agency MBS at a cheap cost. However, the question regarding VMBS is why buy agency MBS when there are better alternatives with higher yields, near-zero duration, and similar credit quality? Due to this, I am initiating VMBS on Seeking Alpha as a hold. The opportunity cost relative to securitized credit alternatives (such as AAA CLOs) is too large for me to ignore. That isn’t to say agency MBS is bad for existing holders collecting the carry, but I would not initiate new positions here.

The Positives for Agency MBS

The macro environment for agency MBS has improved meaningfully as of late. Quantitative tightening ended on 12/01/2025, after reducing agency MBS by approximately $600 billion since June of 2022. MBS' runoff still continues at a smaller scale, at about $15-$20 billion a month, which is below the $35 billion cap.

Additionally, President Donald Trump had directed FNMA and Freddie Mac to purchase approximately 200 billion in agency MBS, and the combined GSE portfolios grew by $10.5 billion in February of 2026, with Fannie and Freddie ramping up purchases as spreads widened in March. Spreads have settled to about 200 bps over Treasuries.

Bank demand is returning as well due to a steeper yield curve and the Federal Reserve softening Basel III requirements, which are creating conditions for banks to step back into MBS, albeit cautiously. This is a touchier topic of discussion, following the disaster regarding banks and MBS in 2008, but I would like to note that even with the Basel III reduction, reserves are still twice as high as they were in 2008, with the mortgage market being drastically more regulated than what it was prior to the GFC.

The Problem

MBS posted its best annual return since 2022 within 2025. Spreads have tightened from their recent wide points in 2023, and I believe the big move in the mortgage market is behind us. In their 2026 fixed income outlook, Cambridge Associates noted that while MBS spreads remain above the historical averages (unlike IG corporate debt, which has compressed below the average), the sector has a structural disadvantage, with convexity limiting how much of the spread that investors can capture.

Agency MBS has negative convexity. Meaning, the upside is capped, while the downside is not. If borrowing rates were to fall 100 bps, VMBS does not give you price appreciation in the way a longer-duration treasury option would. Borrowers refinance, and the investor becomes subject to prepayment risk; principal returns to investors at par value, duration extends, and you face reinvestment risk at lower yields. If borrowing rates were to rise 100 bps, nobody can refinance; duration extends yet again, and the investor eats the full price decline (as newer debt is being issued at higher rates). A +300-bps shock to the rate market on an average 5.5% MBS coupon would tank price by approx. 15%. Unlike the pandemic-era 2% MBS coupons, we currently do not have the cushion of deep discount pricing either. While a 300-bps shock is not likely, it is not impossible and should be considered when debating agency MBS in a portfolio as a diversified fixed income option.

The fact of the matter is that with the evolution of the ETF market, you can own floating-rate fixed-income vehicles with near-zero duration and a comparable credit quality. Accepting the asymmetric payoff requires a meaningfully higher yield to compensate investors, and unfortunately, VMBS does not offer enough yield for the relative risk and uncertainty it brings to the portfolio.

Alternatives for New Positions

Metric VMBS (Agency MBS) JAAA (AAA CLOs) JA (AA-A CLOs) JBBB (BBB CLOs)
Yield/YTW ~4.15% 5.03% ~5.10% 6.86%
Effective Duration ~5.0 years 0.09 years ~0.1 years 0.08 years
Convexity Negative Positive Positive Positive
Rate Structure Fixed Floating (SOFR +) Floating (SOFR +) Floating (SOFR +)
Credit Quality Agency guarantee AAA (zero historical defaults) AA to A BBB (low default)
Expense Ratio 0.03% 0.20% ~0.25% (est.) 0.47%
AUM $14.9 billion $26.9 billion $100 million (seed) ~$1.1 billion
1-Yr Net Flows -$4.5 billion +$5.5 billion New (Feb 2026) +$1.6 billion
Prepayment Risk Significant None None None
Inception 2009 2020 2026 2022

The comparison between these should make MBS buyers in the present day more cautious about adding to existing MBS positions. A superprime CLO ETF (JAAA) yields 88 bps more than VMBS, carries essentially no duration risk compared to the 5 years of rate exposure of VMBS, and has positive convexity vs. VMBS’s negative. Please see my article on JAAA for a more in-depth review of Janus’s superprime CLO ETF. For investors who want to step down the CLO tranche for a higher yield, Janus Henderson also launched their AA-A CLO ETF (JA), which offers an approx. 5.1% YTW in the AA-A tranche, while JBBB delivers a 6.86% YTW in the BBB space with a 0.47% expense ratio. The CLO ETF menu now offers the entire investment-grade spectrum for investors. The only place where VMBS seems to win is the expense ratio, which is 0.03%. The fee differential can have an effect over decades but does not offset an 80+ bps yield disadvantage and a superior risk-adjusted return compared to that of VMBS.

A Necessary Discussion on Structured Debt: MBS, CDOs, and CLOs — a Lesson from 2008

Within my graduate and doctoral studies, in addition to my time as an IAR and my posting of structured debt on Seeking Alpha, any discussion of structured credit brings about the conversation of the ghost of 2008: The GFC. It is worth noting that CLOs are not the CDOs of 2008. While the acronym is very similar, the financial instrument is not. Additionally, any time I get to talk about 2008, I will jump on the opportunity, as I spent a lot of time in my studies focused on fixed income (as well as the GFC).

Agency MBS (The holdings of VMBS) are Mortgage-Backed Securities wrapped by one of the three government entities: GNMA (Ginnie Mae), FNMA (Fannie Mae), and FHLMC (Freddie Mac). GNMA carries the explicit full faith and credit of the U.S. government (the way treasuries do), while FNMA and FHLMC carried an implicit guarantee that became explicit when both were placed into federal conservatorship in September of 2008. The credit risk on the underlying is absorbed by the agency instead of the securities holder. In 2008, while the entire structured credit scene was imploding, agency MBS holders did not take a single credit loss. The GSEs absorbed defaults, the treasury backstopped GSEs, and the bonds were paid in full. The risks associated with agency MBS are prepayment and duration risk instead of default risk. What blew up in 2008 was subprime RMBS without GSE backing, where the cash flows depended solely on borrower repayment. These are NOT in VMBS.

CDOs Are Collateralized Debt Obligations

In the pre-crisis form, they were predominantly filled with subprime residential mortgages, and even worse towards the tipping point, by tranches of OTHER CDOs (the infamous CDO-squared). The collateral was correlated, retail-credit driven, and static. When U.S. home prices fell nationally for the first time since the Great Depression, the entire correlation matrix went to one. AAA tranches that models said would never default, defaulted. The structure failed, as the collateral assumptions were wrong (the housing market can never go down).

CLOs Are Collateralized Loan Obligations

CLOs are filled with senior secured corporate leveraged loans, which are first lien claims on businesses with cash flow, across 150-300+ different issuers, spanning every sector of the economy. Unlike pre-crisis CDOs, the collateral is sector diversified, actively managed by a CLO manager who trades in and out of names, and structurally subordinated such that the AAA tranche sits behind 35-40% of equity and mezzanine debt cushioning to absorb losses. To wipe out an AAA CLO tranche, you would effectively need a worse default rate than 2008 or worse than anything observed post-WWII.

Technically, a CLO is a CDO. CDO is the broad umbrella term for any structured debt instrument that pools cash-flowing debt and tranches them by seniority. CLOs (corp. leveraged loans), CMBS (commercial mortgages), RMBS (residential mortgages), ABS CDOs (Asset-Backed Securities within collateralized debt obligations, AKA the toxic CDOs of 2008), and synthetic CDOs (CDS – Credit default swaps written on referenced 2008 CDOs that investors collected insurance-like premiums that mimicked the cash flows of real MBS, thus the term “synthetic”) all live under the umbrella of the term “CDO.” The reason that this matters is that the umbrella term of a CDO became the colloquial shorthand for the MBS CDO crisis that shook the financial system in 2008, which were baskets of subprime mortgages (RMBS), into additional baskets of CDOs, and then the worst from those, and putting them into even more baskets (CDO-squared). When an investor hears the term CDO, it is important to ask, “The CDO of what?” The answer determines whether you are looking at a very speculative investment vehicle or an investment vehicle that financed the leveraged loan market through three financial crises without a single default. As an investor, knowing what is in the basket before you decide what you think is important to making an informed, unbiased decision.

Risks Affecting the Hold Rating: What Would Change It to a Buy or Sell

Back to VMBS, the hold rating reflects a balanced view of the Agency MBS sector, with adequate carry that does not justify new capital allocations but no acute reason to exit either. The performance of agency MBS remains largely in line with the average returns of fixed income, making it a hold. Several catalysts could move the needle in one direction or another.

What Would Move VMBS to a Buy?

A spread widening to 220 bps over treasuries would make agency MBS and VMBS very appealing. The current 200 bps approximate spread is above the 10-year average but well beneath the 2023 widening. A move back towards 220 bps (which could be triggered by a GSE buying pause or rate volatility) would create a materially better entry into the agency MBS market with more runway. At those levels, the carry and compression case starts competing with CLOs on a risk-adjusted basis, which is an important consideration in my decision-making with fixed income.

Additionally, a genuine recession scenario involving the Fed cutting 200 bps or so over 1-1.5 years if the economy is breaking down causes the thesis to flip. In a recession, leveraged loan defaults rise, and while AAA CLOs sit behind massive subordination protection, BBB and BB CLO tranches are exposed to significant credit risk, and the AAA tranches face price volatility from widening spreads. Meanwhile, agency MBS has zero credit risk exposure regardless of macro conditions. In severe economic downturns, agency MBS becomes a genuine haven that earns a neat bid due to investors flight to safe havens, while CLO spreads widen out. This is the job of the agency MBS asset class in a diversified fixed income portfolio, and part of the reason why it is hard to justify a sell rating, is due to it being a way to hedge exposure to cyclical debt markets.

Additionally, formal FHFA rulemaking on the $200 billion GSE purchase program could move the needle closer to buy. A defined purchase schedule involving size, pace, and duration commitments would convert a soft macroeconomic tailwind into a hard one. The market has not fully priced in the $200 billion purchase program, as the execution has been murky.

What Would Move VMBS to a Sell

If there are sustained outflows from VMBS, I would reconsider a hold and move it to a sell. VMBS has shed approx. $4.5 billion in the trailing 12-month inflows and outflows. If the bleeding continues to happen, bid-ask spreads will widen, tracking error drift will occur, and the 1 bp fee advantage over other competitors in the agency MBS space (MBB) will stop compensating for the disadvantage involving liquidity.

Additionally, if GSE buying stalls or pivots to IPO, it will also result in bad news for VMBS. Due to the GSE buying lacking formal rulemaking and Freddie Mac’s portfolio stalling in recent months, one of three positive tailwinds disappears for agency MBS. Lastly, further compression in CLO spreads while Agency MBS remains flat will encourage a sell rating.

If superprime or prime CLO yields hold near 5% with MBS yields drifting lower to 4%, the question then becomes, “Why own MBS at all?” If the yield disadvantage widens beyond 100 bps, alongside the structural disadvantages, VMBS becomes a strong sell.

Takeaway

VMBS is the cheapest way to gain exposure to agency MBS, and agency MBS does indeed offer a modest value over IG corporate bonds at the current spread levels. The three macro tailwinds are real, but the consensus is that these are largely priced in. The fund's low expense ratio is the best in the peer group. Framed against the broader fixed income asset class, agency MBS is an inferior (but not terrible) risk-adjusted allocation compared to other options, which offer higher yields, lower duration, and positive convexity. Negative convexity is not compensated enough at the current spreads.

Hold VMBS if you own it for the carry, but stay away from initiating new positions. Investors seeking securitized credit with better risk-adjusted returns should look to prime CLOs instead, where the yield, convexity, and floating-rate structure deliver better results within a portfolio across most forward-looking scenarios.

data.title: 2 Of The Most Undervalued Real Assets For The Coming Macro Shift
data.description:
My highest conviction macro bet right now is on the ongoing de-dollarization trend. As a result, my favorite place to allocate capital is in real assets. My highest conviction bets in recent years have been gold (GLD), silver (SLV), mining stocks (GDX), and midstream infrastructure (AMLP). However, all of these sectors have performed quite well since I loaded up on them, leaving fewer bargains available in them for deploying fresh capital.

The good news is that there still remain several real asset opportunities for deploying capital at bargain valuations. In this article, I'm going to detail two of some of the most attractive ones at the moment that also happen to pay attractive dividend yields.

Why De-Dollarization Is Accelerating

Before I get into the specific names, though, I first want to detail why I'm so bullish on the de-dollarization trend.

First and foremost, the U.S. government is running massive, persistent fiscal deficits, and neither party appears to have the political will to meaningfully reverse them. Additionally, recent U.S. foreign policy, whether it be sanctions on Russia after it invaded Ukraine or even its recent trade policy and diplomatic negotiations with some of its leading trading partners, has shaken global confidence in the dollar reserve system and the U.S. as the reliable champion of free trade that it has traditionally been. Thus, many central banks are increasingly moving their reserves out of dollars and into alternatives.

On top of that, China and Russia, along with several other nations, are actively trying to challenge dollar dominance in the settlement of international transactions, and China's rising military and economic might continues to put momentum behind this effort. I see little likelihood of reversing these trends for the foreseeable future, so I expect the de-dollarization trend to continue for years to come, making bets on quality real assets a likely winner over the long term.

Why Real Assets Win In A De-Dollarization World

The reason I am so confident that real assets will be a winner over the long term due to de-dollarization is that currently there are trillions of dollars held overseas from years of U.S. current account deficits that will begin to come back to the United States as foreign institutions and individuals gradually de-dollarize. However, since they are seeking to de-dollarize, they're not going to put that money into Treasuries as they have in the past but instead will be looking for a combination of purchases and real asset investments to put those dollars into.

While there could be some reversing of the trade deficit due to de-dollarization, I also expect that a considerable amount of those dollars will flow into investment assets in the United States. Therefore, high-quality real assets in the United States with limited new supply are likely to benefit significantly. This leads me to my two picks that I'm going to share today.

Pick #1: Timberland REITs

The first one is Timberland (WOOD). The reason I like it so much as a de-dollarization hedge is that research shows that it is generally better correlated to inflation than many other asset classes.

Additionally, the value of timberland grows over time, even if nothing happens, since the trees grow larger and larger and amass timber that can be harvested opportunistically when timber prices rise, thereby effectively giving owners a put on inflation.

Given the scarcity and finite nature of timberland, as well as the fact that, in addition to the cash flow nature of being able to harvest the timber itself, it can be converted into productive uses such as real estate development, mineral harvesting, or even turning it into a solar farm, it is a very attractive investment asset class that has numerous potential paths to value realization and has already proven to be a popular investment for high-net-worth individuals and institutional investors.

Some of the most attractive timberland opportunities right now include Weyerhaeuser (WY) and Rayonier (RYN), both trading at steep discounts to their net asset values. This means that you can effectively buy timberland that is well diversified across the United States and is professionally managed at a steep discount to its private market value. Additionally, they pay out pretty attractive yields, with WY offering a dividend yield of about 3.5% and RYN currently offering a very lucrative dividend yield of about 5%. They both also have strong balance sheets, which gives them significant flexibility to be able to buy back stock at a steep discount to NAV, thereby arbitraging the major disconnect in market sentiment right now to create additional value for shareholders while waiting for the long-term thesis to play out.

Pick #2: Residential REITs

The second real asset sector that I think is very attractively priced right now and stands to benefit from the long-term de-dollarization trend is residential real estate (REZ). Many publicly traded residential REITs (VNQ) currently trade at deep discounts to net asset value, which means that you can buy well-diversified, quality multi-family and single-family assets that are professionally managed at very large discounts to their private market value. I expect that in a de-dollarization scenario, residential real estate, especially well-located multi-family assets, will likely benefit, as it tends to be a strong inflation hedge since it is an essential defensive, and the leases are short-term in nature.

Additionally, multifamily real estate is a very popular institutional investment asset class that should draw significant demand from foreign institutions that are looking to move out of dollars and into real assets that are hedged against the decline of the dollar and provide real-world value. Not only that, but there is also a structural housing shortage in the United States, as it must build over 4 million new apartments by 2035 just to keep up with demand. The rent vs. own premium is 105%, meaning that owning a home right now is much more expensive relative to renting due to very high mortgage rates and high home prices.

Some of the names I like a lot right now include:

  • Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA).

  • Camden Property Trust (CPT).

  • AvalonBay Communities (AVB).

  • Essex Property Trust (ESS).

For those who want to move into single-family real estate, American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) and Invitation Homes (INVH) are also attractive residential real estate investments, as they, too, trade at steep discounts to net asset value. Many of these REITs have yields between 4 and 5% and trade at deep discounts to net asset value while having strong balance sheets and well-diversified and high-quality real estate portfolios.

Risks Worth Knowing Before You Buy

Of course, there are also risks involved, as timberland is sensitive to the housing market, given that wood is used in home construction as well as apartment construction. The very weakness in demand for homes, thanks to high-interest rates, is leading to weakness in home building, which means that market sentiment around timberland REITs may remain suppressed for a while, too. However, at the same time, there has been a recent onset of significant new supply in many multi-family markets, so multi-family leasing spreads are fairly weak at the moment, though these are expected to rebound in the coming years.

Meanwhile, single-family rental REITs, while they are enjoying fairly strong demand from the ownership premium, have high-interest rates and regulatory uncertainty due to Trump's desire to ban institutional ownership of single-family rentals, which are weighing on their equity valuations. They are also suffering from a high cost of debt capital due to elevated interest rates. Therefore, their growth rates have been relatively slow recently.

A Rare Chance To Buy Quality Real Assets On The Cheap

While both timberland and residential real estate may require patience for public market sentiment to turn, you can buy well-diversified, professionally managed, and high-quality portfolios of these real assets in the open market at steep discounts to private market values and with yields of up to 5% in many cases that are also backed by strong balance sheets. Therefore, given my conviction in the de-dollarization trend, I think that for patient investors, these are some of the most attractive risk-adjusted investments right now. As a result, we are loading up on timberland and residential REITs in our high-yielding, below-market beta, and total return outperforming portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Stratasys Q4: Revenue Declines And Profitability Still Eludes
data.description:
Introduction

I wanted to check in on Stratasys Ltd. (SSYS) since they just released their full-year 2025 numbers to see why the company’s share price continues to underperform. Unfortunately, I don’t see a reason to jump in just yet, even as its share price trades at multi-year lows. The big worry of mass adoption remains a concern for me, and the latest results do not ease my worries just yet.

By the Numbers

Revenue for Q4 came in at $140m, down almost 7% y/y, which beat estimates by around $700k. If we take a look at the revenue segment in more detail, we can see that the Americas continues to contribute close to 60% of total revenues, which saw a decline of around 4%; EMEA declined around 3%, while APAC saw a decline of around 4%. So, we can see that all regions have been a little soft, and they have been soft for a while, raising more concerns regarding mass adoption. The main reason for these declines was attributed to longer sales cycles and lower service revenues. They are essentially saying that it was out of their hands.

In terms of profitability and efficiency, Q4 non-GAAP EPS was 7 cents, which beat estimates by a penny. So, we can see that if its adjusted EPS is so little, the company clearly doesn’t make a profit in GAAP terms. Adjusted EBITDA was around $9.2m, or around 6.6% of revenue. In terms of GAAP, the operating loss was around $72.5m, an improvement over last year’s loss of $85.6m. Its bottom line came in at -$104.2m vs. -$120.28m last year. So, profitability held up slightly better than revenues, but we are still not seeing great results overall. The management has done a decent job of controlling costs since its EBITDA has improved.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial situation. At the end of the year, SSYS had around $94.5m in cash and $150m in ST bank deposits, bringing $244m in liquidity against no meaningful debt on the books, except for some operating leases. It is very important for companies not to have a lot of leverage because that means a lot of the resources that could have been used to enhance their operations, since the company is not overleveraged, all the resources will be going towards furthering the growth of the company. That means spending more on research and marketing to get the product into a lot more customers’ hands, as well as making the best product that would be suitable for mass adoption.

Cash from operations about doubled from last year, coming in at $15.14m vs. $7.8m last year. This is a decent improvement, but the cash flow itself seems a bit low. Additionally, its free cash flow still came in negative, at around -$7m.

On top of these results, the management guided revenues to be in the range of $565-$575m, which implies there will be very modest growth for this year. In terms of EPS, the management guided for around 12 cents at the midpoint, which was much lower than what analysts were expecting. Around 24 cents. That is a big miss. So, what this tells me is that revenues may have stabilized, but the margin recovery is much slower than anyone expected.

Overall, the results were not that great. Every Stratasys region is taking a beating, while profitability continues to evade it.

Comments on the Outlook

There are a couple of things I can think of regarding the big hurdles for the company. It clearly is a viable business if they are able to generate over half a billion dollars in revenue. The demand is there; however, the question is, why is this not enough to push into profitability? The company is quite fixed-cost heavy, which means that the revenue growth, not being positive, is clearly not enough to absorb its cost base. In short, the company needs much more demand so its utilization rate increases, so that the costs can be spread across more products produced and gain operational leverage.

It all essentially boils down to one thing, which I mentioned back in January, and that is the mass adoption of the technology. With half a billion in revenues, it is still not enough to go profitable, and I’m not sure if the company can scale effectively in the future without increasing its costs at the same rate or even higher. If unit economics say that for every 10% growth, the company’s costs increase by 10% as well, then that means it is not truly a viable option. The revenue base needs to grow faster than the fixed cost structure. Gross profit needs to expand faster than SG&A and R&D. The company is trying to do that clearly, but since it kind of needs to keep investing in R&D for now, I don’t know when the company is going to see GAAP profits.

The management mentioned that there were timing issues with large production deals, long sales cycles, and delayed customer spending. It seems that the customers are not looking to increase their capex spending, which could point to something a lot more serious than something that may be transitory. The management says that the delays are transitory, and they are just temporary delays, but what happens when they keep getting delayed, and no new orders come through? If the deals keep getting delayed for multiple quarters, I would be worried, especially if revenues continue to decline. At that point, the narrative, I think, would change from timing issues to demand issues. Losing demand is not a good look for the adoption narrative.

I think in terms of positives, I would like to see how the company is going to capitalize on the increased demand from the aerospace industry and the defense sector. Very recently, SSYS was selected for a multi-million dollar contract with the Department of War, which is a positive in the adoption narrative, but I’m afraid that if there are revenue increases, operating expenses and other costs will increase at the same rate, or higher, which will make the viability narrative that much more of a worry. I guess only time will tell.

Current geopolitical concerns in the U.S. and the conflicts could create decent demand for upgrades, which could very much improve SSYS's operations. Bad for the world, but good for the company.

Should you invest in Stratasys?

I, for now, have not found a compelling story to dispel my worries of mass adoption that could lead to scalability and eventually profitability. The company continues to see declines in sales, and profitability remains elusive. I would like to see how the next few quarters progress in terms of sales and earnings. I still truly need to see some evidence regarding the mass adoption of the technology. It seems that the technology fell off when 3D printing disappeared, and because of that, no one cared about it afterwards. The company should have been a lot more proactive a decade ago to convince people that this is the right product for them, so right now, I think that it is going to take a while to convince everyone.

data.title: Nvidia: Singularity In Agentic-As-A-Service Macroeconomy
data.description:
I continue to mark a Strong Buy rating on Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) stock based on its shift from a discrete hardware vendor to the single-seller orchestrator of the Agentic-as-a-Service [GaaS] macroeconomy. Through disaggregating large language model ("LLM") inference through the Groq 3 LPX and Vera Rubin architecture, Nvidia can deliver a 35x leap in tokens-per-watt. This leap is opening up an ultra-premium $150 billion+ inference TAM. At the same time, NVLink Fusion shifts hyperscaler-custom-ASICs from existential risks into captive high-margin ecosystem nodes (through a long-term fabric tax).

Moreover, the NemoClaw OS backs inescapable enterprise software lock-in. But extreme systemic risks remain. My Strong Buy NVDA stock thesis is vulnerable to a Jevons Paradox failure (where token demand becomes inelastic, which can trigger a big hyperscaler CapEx cliff). Apart from that, physical power grid interconnection backlogs and the terrifying thermal physics of scaling Co-Packaged Optics [CPO] for the 2028 Feynman architecture can bottleneck deployments, and that can artificially cap revenue growth even amid huge demand, in my opinion.

My previous coverage ahead of GTC 2026 was focused on Nvidia’s shift from a pure silicon provider to a full-stack AI infrastructure leader. In that article, I analyzed Nvidia's architectural supremacy based on the Rubin GPU, BlueField-4, and NVLink 6, which largely scale up inference performance and expand its TAM into Agentic and Physical AI. At the time, despite strong Q4 FY2026 earnings (with 73% Y/Y topline growth), Nvidia's stock faced cyclical valuation compression (that continues to happen as of now). The article also marked macro-risks like possible hyperscaler CapEx exhaustion, U.S.-China aggressive geopolitical decoupling, and increasing HBM4 memory costs that can compress Nvidia's gross margin.

I used a probability-weighted valuation and set a 2026 target floor price of $224.34. I advised bulls to monitor the $162.54 technical support level as a possible opportunity to average down amid short-term bearish momentum.

Now, post-GTC 2026, Nvidia stock reacted to the upside, as expected, from the support level. In this article, I continue the thesis with new positives like Prefill-Decode disaggregation, NVLink Fusion as the Ultimate ASIC Tax, OpenClaw + single-seller state of the GaaS OS, and Physical AI + the 1-Billion-x Compute Multiplier for Reinforcement Learning [RL]. Whereas, the new negatives here are the Token deflation and Jevons Paradox Failure, the extreme fragility of the 7-Chip Monolith and CPO Wall, the Edge-Inference cannibalization, and Geopolitical Whack-a-Mole and Grid-Lock limits. Apart from that, the current article has an updated valuation, technicals, and Alpha Protocol Pre-Earnings markers for Q1.

What Can Take Nvidia’s Stock Price Higher?

My strong bullish thesis for Nvidia stock depends on its shift from selling computing hardware to monetizing Intelligence Tokens per Watt through extreme architectural co-design and the orchestration of the GaaS economy. To expand on the bullish catalyst, I want to start with the Prefill-Decode disaggregation.

I find the Groq Acquisition and Rubin Architecture a smart long-term motion. To expand here, Nvidia’s 2026 strategy is the integration of the Groq 3 LPX [Language Processing Unit, or LPU] into the Vera Rubin NVL72 pipeline. LLM inference holds two phases. One is Prefill, which is compute-heavy and memory-capacity bound. And the second one is Decode, which is latency-sensitive and memory-bandwidth bound. It is hard for Traditional GPUs to optimize for both at the same time.

Through Dynamo 1.0 (Nvidia’s new AI factory OS), Nvidia has disaggregated inference. The massive 288GB HBM4 Rubin GPUs handle the KV Cache and Prefill. Whereas the deterministic compiler-scheduled Groq 3 LPUs (that is, packing 128 GB of SRAM and 640 TB/s of scale-up bandwidth per rack) handle the Decode. This hybrid product pipeline delivers a 35x higher inference throughput per megawatt. It opens up an ultra-premium tier of inference that allows hyperscalers to run 2-trillion parameter Mixture-of-Experts [MoE] models at 1,000+ tokens-per-second. This tier is commanding $150 per million tokens (against ~$3 for standard tiers).

This architectural single-seller status alone opens up a $300 billion incremental top line TAM that ASICs and Advanced Micro Devices’ (AMD) MI325X/MI400 may not structurally replicate without a head-to-head LPU and OS integration.

Along with that, NVLink Fusion is the ultimate ASIC tax, in my opinion. I do not agree with Wall Street analysts’ frequent view that points to custom hyperscaler silicon [Alphabet (GOOG) TPU, Amazon (AMZN) AWS Trainium, Microsoft (MSFT) Maia] as Nvidia’s primary existential risk. Now, I am pointing out that Nvidia is making a sharp counter-maneuver through NVLink Fusion. This is spearheaded (strategically) by a $2 billion investment in Marvell (MRVL).

More on this edge, Nvidia is opening its proprietary NVLink interconnect to third-party custom XPUs. Hyperscalers can now build their custom AI chips, but they connect these chips to Nvidia’s broader AI factory spine (Vera CPUs, BlueField-4 STX, and Spectrum-6 switches). So, with all that, Nvidia is transforming its possible disruptors into its own ecosystem nodes. Through Nvidia’s grip on the networking and memory-sharing fabric (NVLink 6 at 260 TB/s all-to-all scale-up), Nvidia extracts a huge fabric tax from every custom ASIC deployed.

Apart from that, I want to add up OpenClaw and Nvidia’s single-seller status of the GaaS (Agentic-as-a-Service) OS. The $2 trillion enterprise software market is shifting from SaaS to GaaS as software licenses are replaced by token budgets for autonomous AI agents. In my observations, Nvidia is advancing in this shift at the root layer. I want to answer the "How?" Nvidia has subsumed OpenClaw (the fastest-growing open-source Agentic-OS with 1.5 million downloads) and enterprise-hardened it into NemoClaw and OpenShell. Through giving away the OS and the Nemotron 3 Super (120 billion parameter NVFP4-optimized) open models, Nvidia is fabricating the software standards. Moreover, every agentic software platform [from Salesforce (CRM) to SAP (SAP)] is now being built on Nvidia's CUDA-X, cuDF, and cuVS libraries (to process structured and unstructured data).

Through locking in the world's developers into NemoClaw, Nvidia backs the moat that the underlying token generation must happen in Nvidia factories to maintain determinism, security, and low latency. As a result, this builds Nvidia's hardware stickiness for the next decade; this is what I am observing.

Finally, Physical AI and the 1-Billion-x Compute Multiplier for RL are major positive-add to my argument. Although LLM pre-training compute growth is flattening, RL and Physical AI are forming a supercycle. Projects like the Cosmos-3-world models and Isaac GR00T-N1.7 need robots to learn/work through physics-based simulation (Omniverse NuRec). Simulating billions of robotic interactions in real-time requires deterministic low-latency physics calculations that (as of now), in my opinion, only Nvidia's tightly coupled CPU/GPU/LPU stack can provide. The real-world data (I mean atom tokens) is infinite against digital text.

The shift from digital gen-AI to autonomous physical agents (robotaxis, humanoid robots, space computing through the Space-1 Vera Rubin Module) can drive compute intensity up by ~1 billion times over the next five years. This backs my stance that the 40% of Nvidia's non-hyperscaler revenue (enterprise, edge, sovereign AI) can quickly outgrow the cloud segment.

What Can Take Nvidia’s Stock Price Lower?

A 21.4x forward P/E suggests that Wall Street may hold the stance that the $1 trillion revenue target is a floor. Theoretically, NVDA bulls can believe there are possibilities of P/E expansion. However, my stance is that I should not ignore the core long-term macroeconomic and physical risks that can rupture this valuation.

I want to begin with the Token Deflation and Jevons Paradox Failure. Nvidia’s core value driver for CSPs is driving down the cost per token by orders of magnitude (35x with GB300 NVL72). My strong bullish thesis is based on the assumption that the Jevons Paradox will hold. As Compute gets cheaper, demand will grow exponentially to offset the price drop. But, if the utility of enterprise AI agents plateaus (I mean enterprises realize they only need "good enough" reasoning instead of AGI-level logic for daily tasks), token demand will become highly elastic.

If so, hyperscalers will find themselves with gigawatts of Vera Rubin capacity generating tokens that cost mere fractions of a cent, but without the corresponding end-user demand to absorb the volume. This can trigger a brutal CSP margin collapse that can lead to a sharp CapEx cliff post-2027. If hyperscalers halt orders, Nvidia's top line may contract in large %s in a single fiscal year.

Then, there is the extreme fragility of the 7-chip monolith and the CPO wall. To expand, Nvidia’s 2026 AI Factory needs the near-perfect configuration of 7 distinct silicon components. These components are Rubin GPU, Vera CPU, Groq 3 LPU, CX9 SuperNIC, BlueField-4 DPU, NVLink Switch, and Spectrum-6 Ethernet. This leads to a huge supply chain bottleneck focused on TSMC's CoWoS packaging and advanced nodes. As the Rubin Ultra design was already downgraded from a 4-die to a 2-die package due to complexity. Furthermore, the 2028 Feynman architecture depends on Co-Packaged Optics [CPO] to bypass the 1-meter physical limit of copper NVLink. CPO at a 1-gigawatt scale introduces huge thermal expansion and optical alignment issues. A single yield issue in the Groq SRAM integration/a thermal degradation in the Feynman CPO can halt the delivery of entire racks when the average selling price of Vera Rubin is $7 million per rack (total cost per rack can go very high with other components of the AI factory).

Back to my point that Nvidia is no longer selling chips, it is selling synchronized factories. A delay in one component crashes the revenue recognition of the entire cluster, so the risk is becoming larger with the pipeline advancement.

Apart from that, the Edge-Inference Cannibalization (the DGX Spark Effect) is also a big issue for my thesis. Nvidia’s progress in building hyper-efficient models (Nemotron 3 Nano 4B, Mistral Small 4) and local platforms (DGX Spark desktop supercomputers and RTX AI PCs) can cannibalize its own supremacy in data centers. With 128 GB of unified memory on a local DGX Spark desktop and OpenClaw running perfectly on the edge, enterprise developers can execute highly privacy-secured agentic workflows without pinging the cloud. If a large %age of inference (that CEO Jensen Huang hopes will be 99% of future compute) shifts to local RTX PRO 6000 hardware and edge devices (Jetson Thor), the multi-billion-dollar hyperscaler deployments will be deemed overkill. Although Nvidia sells the edge hardware, the margin and revenue per unit are exponentially lower than selling a $3 million+ Vera Rubin NVL72 rack to AWS.

Finally, geopolitical Whack-a-Mole and grid-lock limits cap the upside possibilities for Nvidia's stock price. To clarify, more on how? Despite Nvidia's victory in getting the H200 approved for China with a 25% tariff, the geopolitical ecosystem is actively bypassing U.S. hegemony, in my opinion. Chinese entities like ByteDance are currently building $2.5 billion, 36,000-chip B200 clusters in Malaysia (via Aolani Cloud) to circumvent export controls. If the U.S. Commerce Department implements company-specific global caps (as drafted in late Q1 2026), Nvidia's shadow revenue from the East will be compressed (like what happened to China-side revenue). Although the Emerald AI / DSX Flex partnership attempts to turn AI factories into grid-responsive assets, the ground reality is that the U.S. interconnection queue has a $300 billion backlog.

So, logically, Nvidia cannot deploy a 1 Gigawatt Vera Rubin factory on a grid that takes four years to approve a substation. All in all, the physical reality of transformers and power transmission lines sets a hard cap on Nvidia’s deployment rate and sharply caps its short-term earnings growth irrespective of demand.

Valuation Of Nvidia’s Stock Through Agentic Factory Token-Yield [AFTY] DCF Model

To value Nvidia stock in Q1-FY2027 using traditional hardware semiconductor frameworks (P/E, EV/EBITDA) is kind of old-way for me. Why? Ok, I see this is a big epistemological error on the Wall Street side.

As of now, Wall Street values Nvidia at a 21.39x forward P/E (based on FY27 consensus EPS of $8.29). This multiple indicates that Wall Street still sees Nvidia as a cyclical hardware vendor that is nearing the peak of a super-cycle. But this is incorrect, fundamentally. Nvidia has gone beyond selling discrete silicon, as it is selling Agentic AI Factories. I consider Nvidia as the single seller of Energy-to-Intelligence Refineries. Therefore, for me, the only accurate way to value Nvidia is through an Agentic Factory Token-Yield [AFTY] DCF Model. This method values Nvidia stock based on the global demand for Compute-Joules-per-Token and the software monopolies (NemoClaw and CUDA-X) that lock in recurring factory upgrades.

First, I am reverse-engineering the current $177.39 price to break down what the current $4.31 trillion mCap is pricing in. The consensus reality (for FY2027-FY2031) is that for FY2027 (guidance/consensus) estimate for revenue is $369.42 billion, and EPS is at $8.29 with a Gross Margin of 75%. With that, I am using Jensen Huang’s core metric of $1 Trillion+ firm purchase order visibility for Blackwell + Rubin through the end of CY2027 (meaning FY2028). If I run a DCF (based on FCFE) to reverse-engineer the current mCap based on the cost of equity of 24.16% and 24.9% long-term FCFE margin. This gives a theoretical terminal growth rate of 22.15% that is nearly half of Nvidia’s 10Y Revenue (45.7%) and Levered FCF (50.5%) CAGRs (pointing to a continued long-term software/hardware replacement cycle).

Based on this traditional modeling, the current NVDA stock price is perfectly + optimistically priced in the known consensus data. It assumes Nvidia delivers the $1T pipeline flawlessly but assumes growth aggressively decelerates post-2028 as LLM scaling laws plateau.

My point from the above computation is that Wall Street is modeling Compute as a uniform commodity. In my opinion, it fails to integrate AFTY factors like the disaggregation of inference, the token-multiplier effect of Agentic AI, and the possible 1-Billion-x compute demand from Physical AI. In my stance, the Inference Disaggregation single-seller status (Rubin + Groq 3 LPX) builds an Ultra-Premium Token Tier under $300B TAM expansion in ultra-premium inference hardware upgrades. Whereas, the OpenClaw 15x Token Multiplier (Agentic Software Lock-In) scales OpenClaw as the Windows of AI, as Nvidia targets that global token demand scales exponentially with agent population. Then, with NVLink Fusion as the Hyperscaler ASIC Tax, Nvidia is targeting the data center spine and converting its biggest existential risk into a high-margin Fabric Tax. Lastly, Physical AI’s 1-Billion-x Compute Multiplier effect may expand Nvidia’s revenue derived from Enterprise/Industrial/Sovereign-entities way bigger than Hyperscaler revenue by 2030.

Therefore, for the AFTY-DCF target valuation of Nvidia stock, I am factoring in all the above AFTY-bullish catalysts and adjusting the consensus revenue estimates towards the high end of the range for the next 5 FYs with my year-wise logic.

  • FY2027: Beating $369 billion consensus due to immediate Vera Rubin pull-forward,
  • FY2028 Revenue: Full realization of the $1T+ pipeline + Groq LPX integration,
  • FY2029 Revenue: Agentic enterprise deployment + BlueField-4 STX storage upgrades,
  • FY2030 Revenue: Physical AI/Sovereign AI scaling over 40% of revenue from non-CSPs, and
  • FY2031 Revenue: Global Agentic Token Economy maturity.

These revisions provide a Target mCap upside of 32.79% based on an expansion of the current Wall Street Terminal Growth assumption, at 0.5% point towards half of Nvidia’s 10Y revenue CAGR.

However, I am taking extra realistic caution here. I will monitor hyperscaler token-monetization rates and U.S. grid interconnection timelines. The main forces that can drag Nvidia’s mCap upside include the physical limitation of the electrical grid and a collapse in downstream end-user token demand. As long as the Token-Yield remains profitable for Nvidia’s product ecosystem, Nvidia stock can hold the mCap upside.

Technical Analysis of Nvidia Stock

NVDA stock is in a compression zone in between the 5-week and 13-week EMAs. NVDA stock closed the week at $177.39, above the 5-week EMA at $175.65. This weekly surge post-pivot touchdown means the exhaustion of short-term selling pressure and the initiation of a new accumulation (sequence), in my opinion. The 13-week EMA is a bit above it at $179.23. A close above the 13-week EMA, the stock may go into a squeeze state, as a weekly close above $179.23 may form a short-term trend reversal (a bullish EMA crossover) that can force trend-following market funds to flip from neutral/short to long.

Following the Fibonacci filter, the important technical development is the successful defense of the annual Pivot point at $162.54. The weekly low of $164.27 front-ran this support; that means heavy limit-buy orders were placed by institutional market makers. The $162.54 level is now the absolute (technical) line in the sand for my bull thesis. After having a base at the Pivot, the possible sell-side vacuum on the upside projects a high-probability rotation back to R1 at $210.5. This is in line perfectly with the previous double-top structural resistance. On the downside, if a macroeconomic black swan occurs, then S1 at $114.57 is the main support that was established during the mid-2025 market correction (is the 2026 price floor if trading weakness continues).

Momentum indicators and oscillators ((MACD, RSI, and Stochastics)) indicate that NVDA stock has been entirely reset and neutralizing all previous overbought froth. Like the Full Stochastic is oversold as the oscillator is currently below 20. For a mega-cap, highly liquid, hyper-growth asset like NVDA, a weekly Stochastic at 15.77 is an extremely positive signal for buying, considering the historical pattern. I observed that when NVDA stock’s weekly stochastics compress below 20, it marks a multi-quarter bottom (as seen previously in 2015 and 2022) before massive upward impulses. The %K line is beginning to flatten and is signaling a possible bullish crossover of the %D line.

Whereas RSI is at 49, that indicates total market equilibrium, as of now. Nvidia stock is neither overbought nor oversold on a relative strength basis. It has entirely digested the 2025 run-up, and a push above 50 in the coming week will confirm that bulls have regained control of the momentum. To back this up, the MACD is in negative territory after a multi-week drawdown. But now the MACD Histogram that is at just -0.685 is flattening and preparing to curve upward toward the zero line. This momentum deceleration and the lagging nature of the MACD indicate that the worst of the institutional distribution phase is possibly in the rearview mirror.

Alpha Protocol For Nvidia Stock’s Next Earnings

For the Q1 earnings in late May, Bulls should focus on the three hyper-specific, highly important metrics that back my long-term bull thesis or can trigger the bear case. The first one is the Gross Margin of the 7-Chip Rack. Nvidia guided Q1-FY2027 Non-GAAP Gross Margins at 75%, and Nvidia is shifting from selling isolated GPUs (Hopper) to selling massive, 7-chip synchronized racks (Vera Rubin, Groq 3 LPX, Spectrum-6, BlueField-4). The extreme CoWoS packaging issues with the integration of liquid cooling (DSX Max-Q) and optical transceivers bring in margin-dilution risks. If Nvidia derives a Gross Margin of 75%+, it may indicate that the System-as-a-Product pricing power completely overrides TSMC and supply chain cost inflation. However, a lower margin level may mark that the physical limits of hardware assembly are eroding Nvidia’s pricing power.

Moreover, the second metric is the Non-Hyperscaler Revenue Mix (the 40% threshold). The core bear thesis is the CSP CapEx Cliff. It is the Wall Street fear that Microsoft, Amazon AWS, Google, and Meta will suddenly halt/slow down annual infrastructure spending. At GTC 2026, as per CEO Jensen Huang, Nvidia’s 60% of revenue comes from Hyperscalers (CSPs), and 40% from Sovereign AI, Enterprise, Edge, and Industrial.

On the Q1 call, I will monitor the growth pace of this 40% cohort. If Sovereign/Enterprise/Industrial revenue expands % in the total mix, the CSP Concentration Risk can start to be neutralized. Nvidia stock, here, can have a big multiple expansion as the revenue base will be deemed economically durable by Wall Street (decoupled from Big Tech's CapEx cycles).

Lastly, the third metric is the OpenClaw, NemoClaw, and Enterprise Token Run-Rates. In my opinion, Q1 FY2027 is the first full quarter where enterprise IT has access to Agentic OS (NemoClaw) with the Nemotron 3 Super model (1M-token context window). I will check on the specific metrics on Nvidia NIM monetization and software run rates. If Nvidia is extracting recurring revenue from the $2 Trillion software industry transition, then the software and services run rate will expand considerably in Q1. As a result, Wall Street may possibly re-rate Nvidia stock as a high-margin SaaS/GaaS hybrid asset.

_

All in all, Nvidia stock continues to be a Strong Buy as it is mispriced as a cyclical semiconductor stock instead of the apex sovereign utility comp. The possible 1-billion-x compute multiplier based on Physical AI and robotics (Cosmos 3, Isaac GR00T) backs that Nvidia’s non-hyperscaler revenue can exponentially edge out legacy cloud CapEx. This can completely push ahead Nvidia from the Big Tech concentration risks. To keep a check on my thesis, I will monitor the Q1 FY27 Non-GAAP Gross Margin defense, the pace of % expansion of non-hyperscaler revenue mix, and the software and services run-rate. Apart from that, checking on DSX Flex power-grid interconnection timelines is also important to assess the shifting state of Nvidia's growth cap beyond silicon manufacturing to electricity transmission.

data.title: Top 50 High-Quality Dividend Growth Stocks For April 2026
data.description:
High-Quality Dividend Stock Investable Universe

On September 1, 2024, I started tracking an investable universe of what I believe to be 50 high-quality dividend growth stocks. You can find out more about the formation of this investable universe in the original article.

The goal for this investable universe is to identify high-quality companies and track them to identify opportune entry points. As share prices swing up and down and business fundamentals change daily, my custom tracking tool updates the valuation ratings for each stock and helps me figure out where to focus my attention.

The investable universe as a whole started 2026 on a positive note, with all 50 stocks providing a positive total return of 4.06%. That positive momentum extended into February, with the universe finishing the month with another positive gain of 2.66%. However, this positive start to the year ended in March with the universe declining by 7.28%. For comparison, the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) also declined in March but by a smaller percentage, 4.93%. Meanwhile, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) started the year very strong but was unable to muster a positive gain in March either, the ETF declined by 2.62%. Here are cumulative returns since 1/1/25:

Investable Universe: +9.50%.

SPY: +11.88%.

SCHD: +14.27%.

In a matter of 3 months SCHD has eclipsed SPY and my universe. It'll be interesting if the ETF can hold onto this lead throughout the remainder of the year.

Here are the top 10 performers from the universe in 2026:

  1. EOG Resources (EOG) +39.00%
  2. Applied Materials (AMAT) +33.15%
  3. Lam Research (LRCX) +24.96%
  4. Old Dominion (ODFL) +24.78%
  5. KLA Corporation (KLAC) +21.33%
  6. Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR) +20.84%
  7. ROSS Stores (ROST) +20.51%
  8. The Toro Company (TTC) +19.20%
  9. Fastenal (FAST) +16.26%
  10. Hershey (HSY) +14.99%

Below is an update for this investable universe as of April 6, 2026.

Investable Universe

Let's start with a snapshot of the full investable universe, including share price as of 4/6/26, current valuation rating, forward rate of return estimate, the valuation rating from 3/2/26, and whether or not it has changed.

My investment objective is simple: I want to identify "High Quality" businesses, and I want to buy them at a reasonable price when they present a strong future return potential. I use this investable universe, along with the custom Free Cash Flow valuation model, to narrow my focus.

11 out of the 50 stocks in my universe saw their valuation rating change since 3/2/26. 10 of these changes were favorable, with just 1 being a downgrade. This was not a surprise with the stock market selling off valuations typically improve. Here is a truncated view of just the companies that had their valuation rating change.

33 out of the 50 stocks in the universe currently present an attractive future return estimate of at least 10%. 24 of these stocks also appear to be potentially undervalued based on my Free Cash Flow model. 2 stocks appear to be marginally overvalued, with 7 potentially grossly overvalued yet still attractive based on future growth projections.

The valuation ratings are purely driven by "acceptable" buy price ranges determined with a formula that doesn't always correlate with the potential valuation. For example, Cintas (CTAS) is fairly valued in the FCF model; however, due to its attractive earnings growth projection, the acceptable buy range is extended beyond its implied fair value.

Let's take a closer look at all 33 stocks listed above to give you a better sense of their valuation and return potential.

Attractive Stocks

ResMed (RMD)

Accenture (ACN)

Paychex (PAYX)

FactSet Research Systems (FDS)

Hershey (HSY)

Eli Lilly (LLY)

NXP Semiconductors (NXPI)

Ferrari (RACE)

Lam Research (LRCX)

Starbucks (SBUX)

Graco (GGG)

Amphenol (APH)

Tractor Supply (TSCO)

Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR)

Qualcomm (QCOM)

KLA Corporation (KLAC)

Dividend Increases

7 out of the 50 companies in this universe increased their dividend rate during the past month.

  • Applied Materials - $1.84 to $2.12 = 15.22% increase.
  • DICKs Sporting Goods - $4.85 to $5.00 = 3.09% increase.
  • Garmin - $3.60 to $4.20 = 16.67% increase.
  • ROSS Stores - $1.62 to $1.78 = 9.88% increase.
  • TJX Companies - $1.70 to $1.92 = 12.94% increase.
  • William Sonoma - $2.64 to $3.04 = 15.15% increase.
  • Watsco - $12.00 to $13.20 = 10.00% increase.

Final Thoughts

To reiterate, the objective of this investable universe is to help me identify which of the screened stocks may be an opportune investment at the moment. While all 50 of these stocks are potentially high-quality businesses, it makes more sense to focus on the companies that have stronger track records, appear reasonably or attractively valued, and present a desirable future rate of return.

While this list focuses on dividend-paying stocks, the primary objective is on total return. My investing strategy focuses on achieving a lucrative long-term total return while also accumulating a growing passive dividend stream along the way.
